claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not in local authority care

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex, who is 6, and Blake, who is 16. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! You have two children - Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 16). 

Let me ask you some questions to assess your eligibility for each child.

First, do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't actually. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since you're living abroad, this affects your eligibility.

Can you tell me which country you're currently living in? And are you or your partner a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of HM Forces serving abroad)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not a Crown servant, no. Just living abroad as a regular person.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Since you're not a Crown servant, I need to know which specific country you're living in to determine if you might still be eligible for Child Benefit while abroad.

Which country are you currently living in?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm currently living in Spain.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. You're living in Spain and are not a Crown servant.

Now I need to understand your residency and work status. Do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or are you an EEA or Swiss national, or a family member of one?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't have any of those statuses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me ask you about your children's circumstances to complete the assessment.

Regarding Blake, who is 16 years old - is Blake currently in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is still in education - doing A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Now let me ask about your relationship with your children.

Do both Alex and Blake live with you in Spain, or do they live elsewhere (for example, with another family member in the UK)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't live with me. They both live back in the UK with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. So both Alex and Blake live in the UK with their other parent, while you live in Spain.

Now, are you contributing financially towards Alex and Blake's upkeep? For example, do you send money or provide things like clothes, food, pocket money, or contribute to their accommodation costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute. I help out with both of them - I send money for things like clothes and their other needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To assess whether your contributions would qualify you for Child Benefit, I need to understand the amount.

Do you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (or the equivalent value) towards each child's upkeep? Currently, Child Benefit is £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for additional children. So for two children, that would be a total of about £42.55 per week or approximately £185 per month combined.

Are your contributions at least this much in total for both children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, it's not quite that much. For Alex, I send about £20 a week, and for Blake I send £30 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those specific amounts. So you're contributing £20 per week for Alex and £30 per week for Blake.

One final question: Is the other parent (who lives with the children in the UK) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake. Here's why:

**For Alex (age 6):**
- While you contribute £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, there are two key issues preventing your eligibility:
  1. You're living in Spain and are not a Crown servant, and you don't have EU Settlement Scheme status or other qualifying residency rights that would allow you to claim Child Benefit while abroad
  2. Most importantly, **only one person can get Child Benefit for a child**, and Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**For Blake (age 16):**
- Although Blake is in approved education (A-levels) which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue, and you contribute £30 per week towards their upkeep, the same issues apply:
  1. You're living in Spain without the necessary residency status to claim Child Benefit from abroad
  2. **Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child**, and Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake

The fundamental rule is that two people cannot claim Child Benefit for the same child. Since the other parent is already receiving Child Benefit for both children, you cannot make a separate claim, even though you're contributing to their upkeep.

If circumstances change in the future (for example, if the other parent stops claiming), you would need to reassess your eligibility at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old and lives in the UK with their other parent. While the claimant contributes £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, they are living abroad in Spain and are not a Crown servant or in certain eligible countries. The claimant does not have settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, nor are they an EEA/Swiss national or family member of one. Most critically, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant does not live with Alex and cannot claim Child Benefit while another person is already receiving it for the same child.

Blake is 16 years old, lives in the UK with their other parent, and is in approved education (doing A-levels). While the claimant contributes £30 per week towards Blake's upkeep, they are living abroad in Spain and are not a Crown servant or in certain eligible countries. The claimant does not have settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, nor are they an EEA/Swiss national or family member of one. Most critically, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. The claimant does not live with Blake and cannot claim Child Benefit while another person is already receiving it for the same child.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is living in Spain (not as a Crown servant) and does not meet the criteria for claiming Child Benefit while abroad. They do not have EU Settlement Scheme status or other qualifying residency status. Both children (Alex, age 6, and Blake, age 16 in approved education) live in the UK with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Since the other parent is already claiming, the claimant cannot also claim Child Benefit for either child, regardless of their financial contributions. The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake.

The claimant does not live with Blake and cannot claim Child Benefit while another person is already receiving it for the same child.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is living in Spain (not as a Crown servant) and does not meet the criteria for claiming Child Benefit while abroad. They do not have EU Settlement Scheme status or other qualifying residency status. Both children (Alex, age 6, and Blake, age 16 in approved education) live in the UK with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Since the other parent is already claiming, the claimant cannot also claim Child Benefit for either child, regardless of their financial contributions. The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake.
